target.title: [codex] make workspace prompt routing selectable
target.body:
## Summary This rebuild turns the Codex workspace prompt routing from a forced token-efficiency change into an explicit, user-selectable prompt-surface policy. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.workspacePromptSurface`: - `per_turn_context` (default): quality-first legacy behavior. `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain fresh current-turn context. - `thread_developer`: opt-in token-efficient/high-priority mode. Only stable profile files (`SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`) move to Codex thread developer instructions. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`: - `soul_when_present` (default): when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered, send Codex `personality: "none"` and let the OpenClaw bridge point personality/tone/voice at `SOUL.md`. - `codex`: keep Codex's native personality overlay. - `none`: always disable Codex's named native personality overlay. - Keeps `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` native to Codex project-doc loading instead of duplicating them in OpenClaw prompt text, while fingerprinting their root workspace content so existing OpenClaw bindings rotate when the docs change. - Removes the lightweight Codex `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression so cron/lightweight Codex runs preserve exact user input without disabling native project docs. Lightweight exact-command runs also do not disable Codex's native personality just because `SOUL.md` exists on disk; the SOUL override applies only when SOUL context is actually delivered. - Adds binding metadata/fingerprints so stale native Codex threads rotate when prompt surface, personality mode, native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. ## Why this PR exists The earlier Codex prompt separation work was right to stop mixing PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and native Codex prompt assumptions. Native Codex should keep its own base/model contract, while OpenClaw should add only the host-runtime bridge it owns: message delivery, dynamic OpenClaw tools, ACP/OpenClaw delegation boundaries, and runtime/channel context. The next optimization moved `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `USER.md` out of every turn and into Codex developer instructions. That was attractive for token efficiency, but the adversarial review found a real quality/regression risk: users edit soul/profile/memory files during long-running OpenClaw sessions, and native Codex thread developer instructions are not a per-turn live-reload surface. Once a native Codex thread is already bound, resume-time developer/personality changes can be ignored by the loaded thread unless OpenClaw intentionally rotates the binding. The final direction here is: quality is the default, token efficiency is a choice. ## Prompt shape ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions"] --> B["Codex native personality slot"] B --> C["OpenClaw Codex developer bridge"] C --> D{"workspacePromptSurface"} D -->|"per_turn_context (default)"| E["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
MEMORY / active BOOTSTRAP
fresh current-turn context"] D -->|"thread_developer (opt-in)"| F["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
Codex thread developer instructions"] E --> G["Current user request"] F --> G H["AGENTS.md / AGENTS.override.md"] --> I["Codex native project-doc loader"] I --> G J["HEARTBEAT.md"] --> K["Heartbeat collaboration-mode pointer"] K --> G ``` The bridge text is intentionally narrow. It says OpenClaw is the host runtime and Codex is the native execution harness, that OpenClaw may prepend current-turn workspace/memory/skill/channel context, and that this context is reference material for the current turn rather than a replacement for Codex system/developer instructions. It also explicitly avoids treating every OpenClaw turn as coding work. When non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered and `personalityMode` is left at the default, Codex receives the protocol enum `personality: "none"`. This is not model-visible text that says "have no personality"; Codex's protocol describes `none` as an empty native personality placeholder. The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md` via the OpenClaw bridge. Empty placeholder SOUL files keep Codex's native personality behavior. ## What we learned ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Original legacy behavior
fresh per-turn soul/profile context"] --> B["Prompt separation
Codex gets Codex lane, PI gets PI lane"] B --> C["Token optimization
stable files moved to developerInstructions"] C --> D["Adversarial finding
developerInstructions are not reliable live-refresh for existing native threads"] D --> E["This PR
default legacy quality + opt-in thread developer mode + binding rotation"] ``` Key findings from the review: - `SOUL.md` is personality, not collaboration mode. `collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` is too strong and mode-oriented for general soul/profile content; it remains appropriate for heartbeat/cron pointer behavior. - `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` are Codex project-doc concerns. OpenClaw should not duplicate them into turn text, but it should stop suppressing native loading and rotate OpenClaw's native Codex binding when loaded project-doc content changes. - `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skills are volatile enough to remain per-turn context even in the token-efficient mode. - `thread_developer` needs freshness metadata. If users opt into it, profile-file edits must rotate the native Codex thread rather than silently relying on resume-time overrides. - The default should preserve the legacy quality/freshness behavior because token savings should not silently outweigh agent identity quality. OpenAI's Codex prompt write-up describes native project-doc loading with `AGENTS.override.md`, `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback filenames, and the `project_doc_max_bytes` cap: https://openai.com/index/unrolling-the-codex-agent-loop/ ## Authority and lifetime | Surface | Owner | Files / content | Lifetime | This PR | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Codex base/model instructions | Codex | Codex system/model stack | Native thread | OpenClaw does not override | | Codex personality slot | Codex protocol | `friendly`, `pragmatic`, `none` | Native thread/turn param | Configurable via `personalityMode`; defaults to `none` only when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered | | OpenClaw developer bridge | OpenClaw | Runtime/delivery/tool/delegation bridge | Native thread | Kept narrow and host-owned | | OpenClaw per-turn context | OpenClaw | Soul/profile/memory/bootstrap/skills/channel context | Every turn | Default quality-first surface | | Codex thread developer instructions | OpenClaw -> Codex | Stable soul/profile files only | Native thread | Opt-in `thread_developer`, with fingerprint rotation | | Native Codex project docs | Codex | `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, fallbacks | Native project-doc load | Preserved; no `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression | | Heartbeat collaboration pointer | OpenClaw | Pointer to `HEARTBEAT.md` | Heartbeat turn | Still separate from soul/profile | ## Scenario coverage | Scenario | Expected behavior | | --- | --- | | Default Codex app-server run with non-empty `SOUL.md` | Stable profile files are current-turn context; Codex native personality is disabled; bridge points personality at `SOUL.md`. | | Default Codex app-server run with empty `SOUL.md` | Empty placeholder content does not suppress Codex's native personality overlay. | | User edits `SOUL.md` mid-session in default mode | Next turn sees the edit because the file remains per-turn context. | | User opts into `thread_developer` | Stable profile files move to developer instructions; `MEMORY.md` and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain turn context. | | User edits `SOUL.md` in `thread_developer` mode | OpenClaw fingerprints the tracked developer files and starts a fresh native Codex thread. | | User edits `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` | OpenClaw fingerprints loaded native project-doc content and rotates the native Codex binding. | | Lightweight cron Codex run | OpenClaw still skips extra bootstrap prompt text for exact command preservation, does not disable native Codex project docs, and does not send `personality: "none"` unless the user explicitly configured `personalityMode: "none"`. | | Existing stale binding from the previous PR | Missing prompt-surface metadata forces a new native Codex thread, so old bindings do not preserve stale developer/user context. | | Manual `/codex resume ` binding | The minimal manual binding resumes once, then persists the current prompt-surface/personality metadata after successful resume. | ## Implementation notes - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.ts` adds the new config enums and resolvers. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` selects the workspace prompt surface, computes the workspace fingerprint, chooses the positive SOUL bridge behavior, and keeps native project docs out of OpenClaw turn text. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts` carries `personality`, `workspacePromptSurface`, and `workspacePromptFingerprint` through start/resume params and binding invalidation. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.ts` persists the prompt-surface metadata. - Docs now describe the architecture in `docs/plugins/codex-harness-runtime.md`, `docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`, and `docs/concepts/system-prompt.md`. ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Codex workspace prompt routing is selectable, defaults to fresh per-turn profile context, rotates stale native Codex bindings when native project docs or thread-developer profile files change, and only disables Codex's native personality when non-empty SOUL context is actually delivered or the user explicitly forces `personalityMode: "none"`. - Current narrowed head tested: `ad037cc543` on branch `codex/workspace-context-routing-v2`, rebased on `upstream/main` `a13468320c`. - Scope note: this head removes the prior fs-safe default flip, archive fallback, Windows ACL policy change, and dependency expectation cleanup from the PR. The only non-Codex file left is `extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts`, a behavior-preserving object-spread lint cleanup required because the repo's fail-safe changed gate lints the extension surface on this branch. - Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`, using a temporary OpenClaw gateway and Codex app-server model route. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` - Evidence after fix: ```text [gateway-codex-live] project-doc-budget-binding-proof { "defaultHiddenEditKeptThread": true, "hiddenEditKeptThread": true, "visibleEditRotatedThread": true, "defaultFirst": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "defaultHidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "first": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "hidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "visible": { "threadId": "019e45f3-6951-7eb0-83ab-5b009ececa6f", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "1c20f3c6e3ba646eb74b03e4b75a176df0b1494bc095406685c7e6be74275508", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" } } Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 1 passed | 2 skipped (3) Duration 94.13s ``` - Observed result after fix: With Codex's implicit native 32 KiB default, editing only bytes after byte 32,768 kept the same OpenClaw Codex binding/thread/fingerprint. With `project_doc_max_bytes = 8`, editing only bytes outside the explicit native budget kept the same binding/thread/fingerprint, while editing bytes inside the native budget rotated both the native thread and workspace prompt fingerprint. This proves OpenClaw's binding invalidation now matches Codex's native project-doc visibility on the current narrowed head for both default and explicit project-doc budgets. - What was not tested: Broad cross-platform and full-matrix coverage are left to GitHub Actions; the live proof used local macOS Codex app-server auth/model access. ## Validation Current-head focused validation from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "project_doc_max_bytes|default project-doc budget" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 3 tests passed, 195 skipped. Current-head live Codex harness proof: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 1 test passed, 2 skipped. Current-head formatting/check validation: ```bash pnpm exec oxfmt --check \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts git diff --check ``` Result: passed. Changed-gate validation: ```bash node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs -- extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts pnpm check:changed ``` Result: passed. `pnpm check:changed` completed typecheck, lint, dependency/package guards, duplicate coverage, runtime sidecar loader guard, conflict-marker/changelog checks, and runtime import-cycle checks. ## Maintainer review notes This PR intentionally keeps the Codex-native lane narrow: - It does not change harness selection policy, model routing, auth routing, or Codex app-server transport behavior. - It preserves native Codex base/model instructions and native project-doc loading instead of replacing them with OpenClaw prompt text. - It keeps quality-first per-turn profile context as the default and makes token-efficient thread developer instructions opt-in. - It rotates native Codex bindings when the selected prompt surface, personality policy, visible native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. - It leaves volatile `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skill context in the per-turn lane even when stable soul/profile files are opted into thread developer instructions.

target.title: fix(control-ui): avoid protected local avatar image URLs
target.body:
## Summary
- stop passing protected same-origin /avatar routes directly into Quick Settings image tags for local assistant avatars
- use the authenticated blob URL when the avatar fetch has completed, and fall back while it is still loading
- add UI regression coverage for blob-backed local avatars and the protected-route loading state

## Real behavior proof
behavior: Quick Settings must not render a protected `/avatar/:agentId` route directly for a local assistant avatar when Control UI auth is token/hash based. It should use the authenticated blob URL produced by the existing avatar fetch path, or fallback while that blob is still loading.

environment: Local OpenClaw Gateway `2026.5.12` on macOS, Control UI at `http://127.0.0.1:18789`, assistant avatar override configured as `assets/nina-ui-avatar.jpg` for `main`.

steps:
1. Read live bootstrap config with bearer auth.
2. Fetch `/avatar/main` without bearer auth.
3. Fetch `/avatar/main` with bearer auth.
4. Run focused patched UI tests for Quick Settings/app render avatar routing.

evidence:
```console
$ curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer <redacted>" http://127.0.0.1:18789/__openclaw/control-ui-config.json | jq '{assistantAvatar,assistantAvatarSource,assistantAvatarStatus,assistantAvatarReason,assistantAgentId}'
{
  "assistantAvatar": "/avatar/main",
  "assistantAvatarSource": "assets/nina-ui-avatar.jpg",
  "assistantAvatarStatus": "local",
  "assistantAvatarReason": null,
  "assistantAgentId": "main"
}

$ curl -sS -o /tmp/avatar-unauth.json -w "%{http_code} %{content_type}\n" http://127.0.0.1:18789/avatar/main; cat /tmp/avatar-unauth.json
401 application/json; charset=utf-8
{"error":{"message":"Unauthorized","type":"unauthorized"}}

$ curl -sS -o /tmp/avatar-auth.jpg -w "%{http_code} %{content_type} %{size_download}\n" -H "Authorization: Bearer <redacted>" http://127.0.0.1:18789/avatar/main
200 image/jpeg 511250

$ file /tmp/avatar-auth.jpg
/tmp/avatar-auth.jpg: JPEG image data, JFIF standard 1.01, aspect ratio, density 1x1, segment length 16, progressive, precision 8, 2816x1536, components 3

$ pnpm --dir ui test src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts src/ui/views/config-quick.test.ts --reporter=verbose
Test Files  2 passed (2)
     Tests  20 passed (20)
```

observedResult: The live setup proves `/avatar/main` is the correct configured local avatar but unauthenticated direct image loads fail with 401, while authenticated fetch returns the real JPEG. The patch prevents Quick Settings from emitting the protected route directly and only renders the authenticated blob URL once available.

notTested: Full browser screenshot automation was not captured because the available OpenClaw browser tool blocked localhost navigation in this cron environment. The live Gateway/avatar route behavior and focused UI regression tests cover the failure path.

## Tests
- `pnpm --dir ui test src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts src/ui/views/config-quick.test.ts --reporter=verbose` (20 passed)

## Review
- Self-review: checked the Quick Settings avatar data path and focused UI diff for scope/secrets/debug leftovers.

target.title: feat(telegram): add progress assistant preview lane
target.body:
## Summary
- add `streaming.progress.assistantPreview` to enable assistant partial previews while staying in `mode: "progress"`
- keep the existing Telegram progress/tool/status draft on the answer lane and stream assistant partial text on a separate transient assistant lane
- clear/reset the assistant preview lane on final answer delivery and new assistant-message boundaries
- regenerate bundled channel config metadata

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram `mode: "progress"` already shows the useful live tool/status/progress draft, but it drops assistant/Nexus-style answer partials. Switching to `block`/`partial` is not acceptable because it replaces the existing progress-live behavior. This patch adds an opt-in second assistant preview lane for answer partials while leaving progress mode intact.
- **Real environment tested:** Mac mini production OpenClaw setup, Telegram enabled/OK, current installed Gateway left untouched at 2026.5.7. Worktree tested at `/Users/thomas/.openclaw/workspace/projects/openclaw-upstream/openclaw-telegram-dual-live` on branch `feat/telegram-progress-assistant-lane`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```text
$ openclaw status --deep | sed -n '1,80p'
OpenClaw status
...
Gateway              local · ws://127.0.0.1:18789 ... reachable 56ms ... app 2026.5.7
...
Channels
│ Telegram │ ON      │ OK     │ token config+tokenFile ... accounts 2/2 │
```

```text
$ node --import tsx - <<'NODE'
import { resolveChannelPreviewStreamMode, resolveChannelProgressDraftAssistantPreview } from './src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.ts';
const currentProductionStreaming = { streaming: { mode: 'progress' } };
const afterPatchStreaming = { streaming: { mode: 'progress', progress: { assistantPreview: true } } };
console.log('environment=Mac mini OpenClaw worktree');
console.log('current.mode=' + resolveChannelPreviewStreamMode(currentProductionStreaming));
console.log('current.assistantPreview=' + resolveChannelProgressDraftAssistantPreview(currentProductionStreaming));
console.log('after.mode=' + resolveChannelPreviewStreamMode(afterPatchStreaming));
console.log('after.assistantPreview=' + resolveChannelProgressDraftAssistantPreview(afterPatchStreaming));
NODE
```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal capture from the patched OpenClaw worktree:

```text
environment=Mac mini OpenClaw worktree
current.mode=progress
current.assistantPreview=false
after.mode=progress
after.assistantPreview=true
```

Supplemental local verification:

```text
$ npx pnpm config:channels:check
$ npx pnpm vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/lane-delivery.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.helpers.test.ts
Test Files  4 passed (4)
Tests       88 passed (88)

$ npx pnpm check:changed
Process exited with code 0.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The patched config resolver keeps the existing production-style `progress` mode unchanged by default (`assistantPreview=false`) and enables the new additive assistant preview only when `streaming.progress.assistantPreview=true`. The Telegram dispatch test covers the actual lane split: progress/tool updates remain on the progress draft and assistant partials update the separate assistant lane.
- **What was not tested:** Production activation against the installed Gateway, real Telegram message delivery from the patched branch, and Gateway restart were intentionally skipped. This PR is meant to be merged/released first; local production activation should happen later via config after release.

## Testing
- `npx pnpm config:channels:check`

- `npx pnpm check:changed`

## Notes
- Existing `off`, `partial`, `block`, and `progress` semantics stay unchanged by default.
- The new behavior is opt-in via `streaming.progress.assistantPreview: true`.

target.title: feat(slack): support per-channel replyToMode
target.body:
## Summary
- add `replyToMode` to Slack per-channel config/schema/types and generated channel metadata
- resolve channel config before account/chat-type fallback when determining Slack reply/thread mode
- pass resolved channel config through the regex-mention reroute path
- document `channels.slack.channels.<id>.replyToMode`
- clean the branch back down to the focused Slack feature diff on current `main`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Slack per-channel `replyToMode` overrides account/chat-type fallback for a real channel-style Slack inbound event. With account/chat-type set to `all` and channel override set to `off`, the prepared reply stays top-level (`MessageThreadId=<unset>`, `replyToThreadTs=<unset>`). The regex mention reroute path keeps the same channel override.

**Real environment tested:** Repo/branch `truiem-bot:slack-channel-reply-to-mode`. Original behavior proof was captured on commit `27f8056aafa8e52b9aaabc7daf2241ab7d2c84d6`; the branch has now been cleaned and replayed onto current `main` at commit `e493daa6f967c9b77ec1287bbdebdbc5411ef866`. Local validation on `e493daa6f967c9b77ec1287bbdebdbc5411ef866` passed after the cleanup. Slack workspace/channel/user ids used from the real installed Slack workspace: `T04URFH9M9C`, `C0B252YDK9D`, `U04VANQ55Q9`. Private names/tokens redacted. No live OpenClaw config was mutated and the gateway was not restarted.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
1. Configure account/chat-type fallback to thread channel replies: `replyToMode=all`, `replyToModeByChatType.channel=all`.
2. Configure channel override: `channels.C0B252YDK9D.requireMention=false`, `channels.C0B252YDK9D.replyToMode=off`.
3. Configure regex mention path: `messages.groupChat.mentionPatterns=[/\bbill\b/]`.
4. Run the PR branch through the production Slack message preparation path:
   ```
   node --import tsx /tmp/slack-channel-reply-proof-run.mts
   ```

**Evidence after fix:** Console output from the command above:
```
REAL BEHAVIOR PROOF: Slack per-channel replyToMode override
config: account.replyToMode=all, replyToModeByChatType.channel=all
config: channels[C0B252YDK9D].requireMention=false, replyToMode=off
config: messages.groupChat.mentionPatterns=[/\bbill\b/]
CASE=implicit-channel-message
team=T04URFH9M9C channel=C0B252YDK9D user=U04VANQ55Q9
input.text="proof: requireMention false channel should reply top-level, not thread"
prepared.replyToMode=off
ctx.ChatType=channel
ctx.WasMentioned=false
ctx.MessageThreadId=<unset>
ctx.SessionKey=agent:main:slack:channel:c0b252ydk9d
route.replyToThreadTs=<unset>
RESULT=PASS channel override won; reply will be top-level
CASE=regex-mention-reroute
team=T04URFH9M9C channel=C0B252YDK9D user=U04VANQ55Q9
input.text="Bill proof: regex mention reroute must still stay top-level"
prepared.replyToMode=off
ctx.ChatType=channel
ctx.WasMentioned=true
ctx.MessageThreadId=<unset>
ctx.SessionKey=agent:main:slack:channel:c0b252ydk9d
route.replyToThreadTs=<unset>
RESULT=PASS channel override won; reply will be top-level
```

**Observed result after fix:** Both cases used `prepared.replyToMode=off` from the channel override even though account/chat-type fallback was `all`. Both cases left `MessageThreadId` and `replyToThreadTs` unset, so OpenClaw would reply top-level instead of threading. The regex mention reroute case also preserved the channel override.

**What was not tested:** I did not deploy this branch into the live gateway or send live Slack messages from the bot, to avoid mutating live config or restarting the user's running OpenClaw gateway during review. Today I did not rerun the original `/tmp/slack-channel-reply-proof-run.mts` proof script because it is no longer present on this migrated host; I did rerun the focused Slack tests, extension type gate, generated-config commands, and whitespace check on the cleaned head.

## Tests
- `git diff --check` — passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-slack.config.ts extensions/slack/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.test.ts extensions/slack/src/threading-tool-context.test.ts` — 3 files, 123 tests passed
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — passed
- `corepack pnpm config:schema:gen && corepack pnpm config:channels:gen` — passed

target.title: [codex] Link heartbeat commitment questions to replies
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes the architectural gap where inferred commitment heartbeats can ask open-ended questions from a proactive heartbeat session, while the user's later answer lands in the target conversation without durable context tying it back to that question.

It now covers both failure modes from the reports:

- Delivered heartbeat questions are mirrored into durable pending-question context on the target session, then drained into the next user-triggered prompt exactly once.
- Inferred commitments get a resolution pass before persistence/delivery, so later same-session completion evidence, including tool-verified completion, can dismiss stale pending follow-ups instead of sending them.

## Root Cause

Heartbeat commitment delivery can happen in an isolated heartbeat run. The visible proactive question is transcript-only delivery state, and replay intentionally filters `openclaw/delivery-mirror` assistant messages before model calls. That means a later user response can be replayed without the assistant question that prompted it unless the transport provides reply metadata.

A second gap existed upstream: extraction could infer an open-loop follow-up before later turns completed and verified the work, but there was no pre-delivery resolution pass against existing pending commitments.

## Approach

- Add `SessionHeartbeatPendingQuestion` / `heartbeatPendingQuestions` to session state.
- Queue a pending question only after a due commitment heartbeat is actually sent successfully.
- Drain pending heartbeat questions only for `trigger: "user"` prompt builds.
- Cache the drained pending-question context alongside plugin next-turn injections for retry stability.
- Teach the extractor to emit top-level `resolved` entries for existing pending commitments closed by the latest exchange.
- Validate resolutions against the current item's `existingPending` dedupe keys and confidence threshold.
- Dismiss matching active commitments before upserting new candidates, and skip creating a candidate resolved in the same extraction item.
- Reserve the new session-entry key so plugin slot projection cannot claim it.

## ClawSweeper / Review Notes

This is intentionally architectural, not wording-only. It avoids depending on Telegram reply context, gives the next ordinary user turn a durable resolution path, and prevents stale inferred commitments from reaching heartbeat delivery when later verified completion has already closed the loop.

The PR branch was fast-forwarded with the resolution-pass commit on top of the existing pending-question fix.

Current PR head: `b329e57e77fbed0b103cefe726a973797dd8d6cc`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Durable pending-question linkage for proactive commitment questions. A delivered heartbeat question is persisted on the target session with source metadata, then drained into the next user-turn prompt context exactly once.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout `/Users/jeeves/openclaw`, branch `codex/commitment-pending-question-context`, head `b329e57e77`, Node v26.0.0 with `tsx`, disposable temp session store under `/tmp/openclaw-pending-question-proof-REDACTED/sessions.json`. No Telegram/network send and no live user session data were used for this proof.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Set `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-proof-missing-config.json` to avoid reading the live installed config.
2. Seeded a disposable `sessions.json` entry for `agent:main:telegram:user-155462274`.
3. Imported the real source functions from `src/config/sessions/pending-question-context.ts` via `node --import tsx`.
4. Called `enqueueHeartbeatPendingQuestion(...)` with redacted May 9-style commitment/source metadata.
5. Read the session store to verify the queued question and source message id were persisted.
6. Called `drainHeartbeatPendingQuestionContext(...)` for the same session and read the store again.

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "enqueued": true,
  "queuedText": "Want me to check your Mega Millions ticket from last night?",
  "queuedCommitmentIds": ["cm_demo"],
  "queuedSourceMessageIds": ["14517"],
  "contextIncludesQuestion": true,
  "contextMentionsInternalSuppression": true,
  "queueAfterDrain": null,
  "tempSessionStore": "/tmp/openclaw-pending-question-proof-REDACTED/sessions.json"
}
```

Observed result after fix: The enqueue path returned `true`, the pending question persisted with the source message id, the drained context included the original proactive question plus the instruction to hide internal heartbeat/commitment machinery, and the subsequent store read showed `queueAfterDrain: null`, proving one-shot drainage for the next user turn.

What was not tested: This proof did not send a real Telegram message or inspect a real lottery ticket image. Physical transport delivery remains covered by the existing heartbeat outbound path; this PR targets the missing durable reply-linkage layer after successful delivery.

Behavior or issue addressed: Inferred commitments are resolved before delivery when later same-session evidence shows the loop is already complete, matching the Gmail reauth false-positive pattern.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout `/Users/jeeves/openclaw`, branch `codex/commitment-pending-question-context`, head `b329e57e77`, Node v26.0.0 with `tsx`, disposable commitment store under `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$(mktemp -d)`. No live installed commitment store and no Gmail account credentials were read.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Set `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` to a disposable temp directory.
2. Seeded the real commitment store API with a pending inferred Gmail reauth follow-up using id `cm_mp4j2jsy_9a9a2f424e` and dedupe key `gmail-reauth:sprichert@gmail.com`.
3. Imported `persistCommitmentExtractionResult(...)` from `src/commitments/extraction.ts` via `node --import tsx`.
4. Persisted an extraction result whose latest assistant turn reported tool-verified Gmail reauth completion and emitted both a repeated candidate and a high-confidence `resolved` entry for the same dedupe key.
5. Reloaded the commitment store through the real store API.

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "proof": "resolved pending inferred Gmail reauth commitment before delivery",
  "createdCount": 0,
  "finalCommitments": [
    {
      "id": "cm_mp4j2jsy_9a9a2f424e",
      "dedupeKey": "gmail-reauth:sprichert@gmail.com",
      "status": "dismissed",
      "dismissedAtMs": 1778752800000,
      "suggestedText": "Did the gog Gmail reauth go through for sprichert@gmail.com?"
    }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: The pending inferred follow-up was dismissed before delivery, and `createdCount: 0` proves the repeated same-key candidate was not recreated in the same extraction pass.

What was not tested: This proof did not run a live Gmail OAuth flow. It exercises the OpenClaw commitment extraction/persistence path that decides whether an already-completed inferred follow-up remains eligible for heartbeat delivery.

## Validation

- `CI=true pnpm test src/commitments/extraction.test.ts src/commitments/runtime.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.commitments.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts`
- `CI=true pnpm check:changed`

target.title: [codex] Add Control UI gateway request timeouts
target.body:
Summary
- Add client-side request timeouts to the Control UI gateway RPC path.
- Keep longer server-declared timeouts alive with grace while timing out unanswered default requests.
- Record bounded CLIENT_TIMEOUT timing and clear timers on responses/flushes.

Verification at current head
- Current head SHA: 95659193b66d613b5d7587f054260f8d87e12298
- CI=true pnpm test ui/src/ui/gateway.node.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
- CI=true pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 ui/src/ui/gateway.ts ui/src/ui/gateway.node.test.ts
- git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD -- ui/src/ui/gateway.ts ui/src/ui/gateway.node.test.ts
- pnpm changed:lanes --base upstream/main --head HEAD --json
- CI=true pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main --head HEAD

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI Gateway RPC client requests now have bounded client-side timeouts, clear timeout timers after Gateway responses, and emit request timing without request params. This proof covers both the successful real Gateway request path and the central unanswered-open-WebSocket timeout path.
- Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw Gateway started from current head SHA 95659193b66d613b5d7587f054260f8d87e12298 on loopback with isolated local state and token auth; real `ui/src/ui/gateway.ts` `GatewayBrowserClient` connected through an actual WebSocket transport using browser-equivalent Origin plus WebCrypto device identity/signing. Additional timeout-specific proof used the same real `GatewayBrowserClient` against an actual local `ws` WebSocketServer that completed `hello-ok`, kept the socket open, and intentionally never answered one request.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started `pnpm openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --port 18977 --bind loopback --auth token --token <redacted> --compact --cli-backend-logs`; checked `curl http://127.0.0.1:18977/healthz`; checked `HOME=<isolated> pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:18977 --token <redacted> --json`; ran a Node/tsx browser-shaped harness importing `GatewayBrowserClient` from `ui/src/ui/gateway.ts` and requesting `health` with a 5000ms timeout. Then ran a second Node/tsx browser-shaped no-response harness at the same head SHA: actual `GatewayBrowserClient`, actual local `ws` WebSocketServer, Origin `http://127.0.0.1:18988`, generated WebCrypto device identity, `connect` answered with `hello-ok`, and `proof.neverResponds` deliberately left unanswered with `timeoutMs: 75` while the socket stayed open.
- Evidence after fix: Copied live output from the real local Gateway/WebSocket/Control UI client run, with token, isolated home path, and generated device identity redacted:

```json
{
  "branchSha": "95659193b66d613b5d7587f054260f8d87e12298",
  "gatewayUrl": "ws://127.0.0.1:18977",
  "origin": "http://127.0.0.1:18977",
  "auth": "token(redacted)",
  "browserIdentity": "generated-and-signed(redacted)",
  "hello": {
    "type": "hello-ok",
    "protocol": 4,
    "role": "operator",
    "scopeCount": 5,
    "hasDeviceToken": true
  },
  "health": {
    "ok": true,
    "pluginCount": 8,
    "channelCount": 0,
    "agentCount": 1
  },
  "timingMethods": [
    {
      "method": "connect",
      "ok": true,
      "errorCode": null,
      "durationMs": 18
    },
    {
      "method": "health",
      "ok": true,
      "errorCode": null,
      "durationMs": 134
    }
  ],
  "clientConnectedBeforeStop": true,
  "closeInfo": null,
  "eventNames": [
    {
      "event": "health",
      "seq": 1
    }
  ]
}
```

Copied live output from the no-response timeout harness, with token and generated device identity redacted:

```json
{
  "branchSha": "95659193b66d613b5d7587f054260f8d87e12298",
  "gatewayUrl": "ws://127.0.0.1:45491",
  "transport": "actual ws WebSocketServer + actual GatewayBrowserClient",
  "browserShape": {
    "origin": "http://127.0.0.1:18988",
    "webCryptoIdentity": "generated-and-signed(redacted)",
    "auth": "token(redacted)"
  },
  "hello": {
    "type": "hello-ok",
    "protocol": 4,

    "scopeCount": 1,
    "hasDeviceToken": true
  },
  "unansweredRequest": {

    "method": "proof.neverResponds",
    "params": {
      "scenario": "unanswered-open-websocket"
    },
    "serverAction": "intentionally left unanswered while socket stayed open"
  },
  "timeoutError": {
    "name": "GatewayRequestTimeoutError",
    "message": "gateway request timed out after 75ms: proof.neverResponds",
    "instanceOfGatewayRequestTimeoutError": true,
    "method": "proof.neverResponds",
    "timeoutMs": 75
  },
  "timingMethods": [
    {
      "method": "connect",
      "ok": true,
      "errorCode": null,
      "durationMs": 1.788
    },
    {
      "method": "proof.neverResponds",
      "ok": false,

    }
  ],
  "timeoutTiming": {
    "method": "proof.neverResponds",
    "ok": false,

  },
  "connectTiming": {

  },

  "connectedAfterTimeout": true,
  "serverMessageSummary": [
    {
      "event": "connection",
      "origin": "http://127.0.0.1:18988",
      "url": "/"
    },
    {
      "event": "message",
      "type": "req",

        "minProtocol",
        "role",

        "userAgent"
      ]
    },
    {
      "event": "message",
      "type": "req",
      "id": "c3605a03-bb26-40f3-894d-0be17a69f6a8",
      "method": "proof.neverResponds",
      "paramsKeys": [
        "scenario"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The real Gateway browser client completed `hello-ok` with protocol 4 as operator, received a device token, successfully read live Gateway `health`, emitted successful `connect` and `health` timings, and the temporary Gateway was stopped afterward. The no-response harness then kept an actual WebSocket open, deliberately did not answer `proof.neverResponds`, and the actual `GatewayBrowserClient` rejected with `GatewayRequestTimeoutError` after the 75ms client timeout while emitting a single `CLIENT_TIMEOUT` timing and remaining connected before stop.
- What was not tested: No deploy, canary, Testbox, release validation, Golden Run success, end-to-end product flow, or MVP score movement. The no-response proof uses an actual local WebSocket server to create the unanswered-request condition; it is not a deployed Gateway canary or release proof.

Historical pre-rebase proof retained for traceability: The earlier real setup proof was captured before the branch was rebased, at SHA e906ef282a95277a4355efd42c2d8ea0ac441d92 on port 18976. It is retained only as historical traceability and should not be treated as the current PR head proof. Historical observed result: the browser client completed `hello-ok` with protocol 3 as operator, received a device token, successfully read live Gateway `health`, emitted successful `connect` and `health` timings, and the temporary Gateway was stopped afterward.

Calibration notes: A query-side CLI attempt without the isolated `HOME` read the operator's real local config and failed on unrelated stale model IDs; rerunning the same health check with isolated local state succeeded. Raw Node socket, missing Origin, and missing WebCrypto identity attempts were rejected during earlier calibration, so the live proof used a browser-shaped client path.

Release evidence and claim ceiling
- This real behavior proof is bounded to a local real OpenClaw Gateway/WebSocket/Control UI client path plus a local actual-WebSocket no-response timeout harness at the current PR head SHA.
- It does not prove deploy, canary, Testbox, release validation, Golden Run success, end-to-end product flow, or MVP score movement.
- No changelog entry is included yet because this branch was intentionally kept to the bounded two-file Card 6 patch.

target.title: fix(gateway): discover disk compaction checkpoints
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #68864.

- discover valid `{session}.checkpoint.<uuid>.jsonl` transcript files when `sessions.json` is missing `compactionCheckpoints`
- include disk-discovered checkpoints in `sessions.list` row count/latest preview and `sessions.compaction.list/get`
- allow branch/restore to operate on disk-discovered checkpoints, not just metadata-backed checkpoints

## Tests

- `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-shims:$PATH pnpm check:changed -- --base origin/main`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`
- `node --import tsx -e '<inline script creating a real SessionManager transcript, checkpoint snapshot, then calling listSessionCompactionCheckpointsWithFilesAsync>'`
- attempted: `corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/gateway/server.sessions.compaction.test.ts -t "discovers checkpoint transcript files" --reporter=verbose` (stopped after the local Vitest/Rolldown build stayed in plugin timing output for over two minutes without reaching test assertions)
- attempted: `corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/gateway/session-compaction-checkpoints.test.ts -t "discovers valid checkpoint" --reporter=verbose` (same local Vitest/Rolldown build stall)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Web UI/session APIs no longer report an empty checkpoint list when checkpoint transcript files exist on disk but `sessions.json` has no `compactionCheckpoints` metadata.

Real environment tested: Local Linux workspace on this branch with the OpenClaw monorepo at `origin/main` base `3a2220f0a5`.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:

```bash
node --import tsx -e 'created a real SessionManager session transcript, captured a .checkpoint.<uuid>.jsonl snapshot, omitted compactionCheckpoints metadata, called listSessionCompactionCheckpointsWithFilesAsync, and required one disk checkpoint with matching leaf/sessionFile'
```

Evidence after fix: Console output from the direct runtime command:

```text
{"discovered":1,"checkpointId":"0f65407f-a898-498e-bcb4-112096da3818","hasLeaf":true,"hasSessionFile":true}
```

Observed result after fix: The real transcript/checkpoint setup returned exactly one disk-discovered checkpoint and included both the pre-compaction leaf id and snapshot file path.

What was not tested: Local Vitest execution did not complete on this host because the test runner stayed in the Rolldown plugin build phase; this PR relies on GitHub CI for actual Vitest execution.

commands.review_summary:

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns, but only when OpenClaw can identify one of its bundled SDK tool-compat normalizers.
- Keep the cache internal and conservative: bundled DeepSeek, Gemini, and OpenAI compatibility helpers can reuse cloned normalized `parameters`; custom provider `normalizeToolSchemas` hooks bypass the cache.
- Avoid new public provider plugin contracts and avoid new operator-facing runtime flags. Tool objects, execute closures, approval state, guards, and runtime policy are still rebuilt per turn.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns can avoid redoing bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization when the provider/model/api/schema signature is unchanged. Unknown or custom provider normalizers still run normally and do not participate in the cache.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a terminal proof with `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against the production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry. The script used the real bundled OpenAI tool-schema normalizer identity through a provider runtime handle, called normalization twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature and different `execute` closures, then repeated the same pattern with a custom provider normalizer.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the current head:

```json
{
  "head": "dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 0, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "afterSecond": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 1, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": { "bypass": 2, "hit": 0, "miss": 0, "store": 0, "size": 0 },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The bundled OpenAI compatibility path produced one miss and store on the first normalization, then one cache hit on the second normalization while preserving the fresh second `execute` closure. The custom provider path bypassed the cache twice and invoked its normalizer twice, proving the cache remains internal to known bundled compatibility helpers.

What was not tested: Full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

## Additional validation

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/build.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime-errors.test.ts

All matched files use the correct format.
```

target.title: feat(embedded-runner): expose prep stage timings
target.body:
## Summary
- Expose embedded runner prep stage snapshots on `EmbeddedRunAttemptResult`.
- Include the same prep stage summary on the `agent_end` hook event for plugin observability.
- Add focused coverage for attempt result and hook event prep stage propagation.
- Document the new optional hook field. No `CHANGELOG.md` change is kept.

## Current upstream status
Latest `origin/main` already has May embedded-runner/subagent performance work and trace logging for startup stages. It still does not expose the attempt prep stage summary to `agent_end` hook observers. This PR is narrowed to that missing diagnostics surface so plugins can collect stage timing evidence without patching core locally.

## Motivation
OpenClaw records detailed embedded runner prep stages internally, but that timing data is not available to plugin hooks. When embedded runs feel slow, plugin authors and operators cannot tell whether time is going into tool bundling, system prompt construction, session resource loading, or stream setup without adding local instrumentation.

Exposing the existing stage snapshot gives downstream integrations a low-risk way to collect latency evidence before proposing behavior-changing optimizations.

## Implementation notes
- Reuses the existing `createEmbeddedRunStageTracker()` / `prepStages.snapshot()` path.
- Keeps all new fields optional.
- Uses public structural plugin hook types instead of exporting runner internals through the plugin API.
- Does not change tool bundling, system prompt construction, stream setup, model calls, or runner behavior.
- Documents `agent_end.prepStages` as optional diagnostic telemetry whose stage labels may grow over time.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Embedded runner prep stage timings are visible to a real non-bundled plugin's `agent_end` hook without changing the embedded run outcome.

Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout, current PR head `8d7ef409272853778bd234da21e777ac880216a6`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, temporary local plugin loaded through `plugins.load.paths` with `hooks.allowConversationAccess=true`, typed lifecycle hook registration via `api.on("agent_end", ...)`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --local --json --session-key agent:default:prep-proof`, and the repo mock OpenAI-compatible server `scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the mock OpenAI server with `SUCCESS_MARKER=OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, ran a local embedded agent turn against a temporary config, and had the temporary plugin write the real `agent_end` event to JSONL. The proof script asserted both the CLI result and plugin hook payload.

Fresh current-head verification:

```text
node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-embedded-agent.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "exposes embedded prep stages" --reporter=dot
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 58 skipped (59)
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the current-head local OpenClaw CLI run and typed non-bundled plugin `agent_end` hook JSONL assertion.

```json
{
  "head": "8d7ef409272853778bd234da21e777ac880216a6",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK",
  "hookSuccess": true,
  "hookHasPrepStages": true,
  "hookTotalMs": 143,
  "hookStageNames": [
    "workspace-sandbox",
    "skills",
    "core-plugin-tools",
    "bootstrap-context",
    "bundle-tools",
    "system-prompt",
    "session-resource-loader",
    "agent-session",
    "stream-setup"
  ],
  "proofDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78381-proof-current.Xjp9Fj"
}
```

Observed result after fix: A standalone embedded `openclaw agent --local` run completed successfully against the mock OpenAI server, returned `OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, and the temporary plugin's real typed `agent_end` handler observed `event.prepStages` with total setup time and expected prep stages.

What was not tested: A live paid external model/provider run was not run. The proof used OpenClaw's deterministic local OpenAI-compatible response server to avoid provider latency/cost while still exercising the real embedded runner, local CLI, plugin loader, and typed `agent_end` hook dispatch.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- Risk: plugin authors might treat stage names as permanent.
- Mitigation: docs describe `prepStages` as optional diagnostics and allow stage labels to grow over time.

target.title: fix: harden subagent completion announce cleanup races
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes a set of subagent completion lifecycle races that can leave completed child runs stuck behind cleanup/announce sequencing or overwrite a terminal error with a later success signal.

The patch makes three related changes:

- Do not block completion announce delivery on browser-session cleanup. The cleanup is now started after the announce/cleanup flow is leased and runs best-effort in the background, with warnings captured instead of blocking the lifecycle path.
- Skip waiting for an active embedded child session when a frozen final reply is already available. If we already captured the reply text, the requester can be notified immediately instead of waiting on a stale/active child run check.
- Preserve a terminal error outcome if a later completion signal reports success for the same run, unless the run was actually restarted after the error. This prevents cleanup/finalization races from turning a failed run into a successful task after the fact.

## Why this matters

The lifecycle path has a few asynchronous pieces that can finish in different orders:

1. child session/browser cleanup
2. completion announce delivery back to the requester
3. task finalization bookkeeping
4. embedded child-run settle checks
5. terminal lifecycle hooks

Before this patch, a slow or stuck browser cleanup could delay the announce/cleanup flow. Separately, if a child run had already produced frozen reply text but still appeared active, the announce flow could wait unnecessarily. And in a failure path, a later `complete` event could overwrite the already-recorded terminal error.

That combination makes subagent completion feel flaky from the user's side: the work is done, but the parent session may not receive the completion promptly, or the persisted final task state can become misleading.

## Implementation notes

### Lifecycle cleanup lease

`completeSubagentRun` now preclaims cleanup when `triggerCleanup` is true and cleanup has not already completed/been handled. That lease is passed into `startSubagentAnnounceCleanupFlow` as `skipBeginCleanup`, avoiding a second `beginSubagentCleanup` race while still protecting duplicate cleanup attempts.

If cleanup is not actually triggered, or announce is suppressed for steer-restart, the preclaim is released so the record does not stay permanently handled.

### Browser cleanup no longer blocks announce cleanup

Previously browser cleanup was awaited before starting the subagent announce cleanup flow. This patch starts the announce cleanup flow first, retires run-mode bundle MCP runtime as before, and then launches browser cleanup as a best-effort async task:

- uses injected `cleanupBrowserSessionsForLifecycleEnd` when provided
- lazy-loads the browser cleanup helper otherwise
- logs warning context on failure
- does not reject or delay completion delivery

### Frozen reply short-circuit

`runSubagentAnnounceFlow` now checks whether `roundOneReply` already contains non-empty frozen reply text. If it does, the flow does not wait for `waitForEmbeddedPiRunEnd` even if `isEmbeddedPiRunActive` reports true. The frozen reply is already the durable completion payload; waiting only increases the chance of a stale active-state race.

### Terminal error preservation

When a run already has a terminal error outcome/reason, a later successful completion signal no longer overwrites it unless the entry's `startedAt` proves the run restarted after the error's `endedAt`. The ended hook and persisted outcome use the preserved effective reason/outcome.

## Tests

Added/updated coverage for:

- announcing without waiting for browser cleanup
- skipping announce delivery when completion messages are disabled
- delete-mode cleanup when completion messages are disabled
- preserving terminal errors against later success completion
- allowing success to replace error only after a restart
- using frozen reply text without waiting on an active embedded child session

Verification run locally:

```bash
pnpm test src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts
```

Result: 2 files passed, 29 tests passed.

target.title: fix(agents): pause yielded main-session runs
target.body:
## Summary

Extend the subagent yield-pause fix from [6f3b5f86](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/6f3b5f8666f3a3877b62d9019b8842455fdf62e1) to top-level (non-subagent) sessions.

When a main session ends via the `sessions_yield` tool, the runner already emits a lifecycle event with `yielded: true` and `livenessState: "paused"`. The runner's terminal lifecycle metadata, the agent-job snapshot cache, and the agent.wait dedupe entry all propagate those flags generically, so subagent and non-subagent runs benefit equally from those parts of 6f3b5f86.

The gap is in `gateway/session-lifecycle-state.ts`. `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` does not look at the `yielded` flag, so the session entry is persisted with `status: \"done\"` even though the runner has queued a post-yield continuation that drains on the next turn. Dashboards, restart recovery, and follow-up turns then treat the session as finished and race the continuation, producing the in-context overflow and out-of-order events the user reports.

## Why the same fix needs to apply to the top-level session

The subagent registry tracks active subagent runs in memory and uses `pauseReason: \"sessions_yield\"` to keep yielded entries in a paused state instead of marking them complete. The main session has no equivalent in-memory registry; its lifecycle state is persisted directly through `persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEvent` into the on-disk session entry. The gap shows up in three places:

1. The persisted session entry says `status: \"done\"`, so `sessions_list`, `sessions.changed` broadcasts, and dashboard UI all show the session as finished.
2. `main-session-restart-recovery` is keyed off `entry.abortedLastRun` and `entry.status === \"running\"`. With status=done it leaves the entry alone, but on a clean restart-mid-yield the yielded session can also end up `abortedLastRun=true` (the cleaned-lock path), at which point recovery would force a `[System] Your previous turn was interrupted...` resume that races the queued continuation.
3. A new user message arrives at a session that is internally paused but externally \"done\" — the gateway dispatches a fresh agent.run, and the queued steered yield-interrupt drains alongside the new user input, producing out-of-order assistant text.

## Shape of the change

- Recognize the lifecycle `yielded` flag in `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` and derive `status: \"paused\"` with `pauseReason: \"sessions_yield\"` instead of `done`. Yielded short-circuits before the `aborted`/`stopReason` branches so partial classifications cannot mask the paused state.
- Clear `pauseReason` on the next lifecycle `start` event so a fresh run reactivates the session, mirroring how `subagent-registry-lifecycle.ts` clears `pauseReason` on completion.
- Add `\"paused\"` to `SessionRunStatus` in both `gateway/session-utils.types.ts` and `agents/tools/sessions-helpers.ts`, and add `pauseReason?: \"sessions_yield\"` to `SessionEntry` and `GatewaySessionRow`.
- Surface `pauseReason` on the `sessions.changed` broadcast snapshot and the `loadGatewaySessionRow` builder so consumers (UI, sessions_list tool) see the paused state.
- Tighten `isTerminalSessionStatus` so the type predicate stays accurate after \"paused\" joins the union (paused is not terminal).
- Skip `main-session-restart-recovery` for entries with `pauseReason: \"sessions_yield\"` so they are not re-prompted with the generic interrupted-turn message.

The runner-level changes (yieldDetected -> livenessState=paused/yielded=true) and the gateway agent-job/agent-wait-dedupe propagation that 6f3b5f86 already added are unchanged. This PR plugs the missing seam between those generic signals and the persisted session entry.

## Test coverage

- `src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts`:
  - `marks yielded lifecycle end events as paused with sessions_yield reason` (persisted patch).
  - `treats yielded as paused even when aborted is set` (yield short-circuit).
  - `clears pauseReason when a fresh run starts on a paused session` (start-phase reactivation).
  - Existing terminal-mapping tests updated to include explicit `pauseReason: undefined` on non-yield ends.
- `src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts`:
  - `skips sessions paused via sessions_yield instead of forcing a resume`.
  - `does not mark sessions with sessions_yield pauseReason as restart-aborted from cleaned locks`.

## Local validation

- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — clean.
- `pnpm test src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/config/sessions.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-list-tool.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.create-event.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent-wait-dedupe.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-output.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/sessions-yield.orchestration.test.ts` — all green (235 tests).
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check`, `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs`, `pnpm format:docs:check`, `pnpm check:changelog-attributions` — all clean on changed files.

## Test plan

- [ ] CI green on \`fix(agents): pause yielded main-session runs\`.
- [ ] Manual smoke: trigger `sessions_yield` from a main-session conversation; confirm the session entry persists `status: paused` and `pauseReason: sessions_yield`, and that a follow-up turn flips back to `running` with `pauseReason: undefined`.
- [ ] Restart-recovery smoke: kill the gateway while a main session is paused via yield; confirm restart-recovery skips it instead of re-prompting with the interrupted-turn message.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Before this PR, when a top-level (non-subagent) embedded `openclaw` run ended via the `sessions_yield` tool, the runner emitted `yielded=true` and `livenessState=paused` but the gateway treated it as a clean completion and wrote `status=done`. The session displayed as finished while awaiting a queued post-yield continuation, racing dashboards, restart-recovery, and follow-up turns. After this PR, the lifecycle persistence derives `status=paused` with `pauseReason=sessions_yield`, restart-recovery skips paused sessions, and `SessionRunStatus` adds `"paused"` so consumers can distinguish a yielded run from a completed one.
- **Real environment tested**: live OpenClaw gateway on rh-bot.lan, pid 65734, build SHA 23804e610c (upgrade-v2026.5.28 with this PR cherry-picked). The deployed `dist/server-chat-*.js` and `dist/main-session-restart-recovery-*.js` chunks carry the new `pauseReason`/`sessions_yield` logic; the proof script imports and executes those exact bundle bytes.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  # On rh-bot.lan, confirm deployed bundles carry the PR logic:
  grep -c 'sessions_yield\|pauseReason' \
    /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/main-session-restart-recovery-CirHJY3p.js
  # -> 1 (the new shouldSkipMainRecovery branch)

  grep -c 'sessions_yield\|pauseReason' \
    /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/server-chat-D9hpq-gf.js
  # -> non-zero (new deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot branches)

  # Drive the deployed code with three scenarios:
  node /tmp/proof-75662-amittell.mjs
  ```
  Proof script (`/tmp/proof-75662-amittell.mjs`) extracts the deployed
  `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` from `server-chat-D9hpq-gf.js` via
  `vm.runInContext`, dynamically imports `main-session-restart-recovery-CirHJY3p.js`
  to grab the exported `markRestartAbortedMainSessions`, seeds a temp
  state-dir with one yielded and one non-yielded `running` entry, and asserts
  three behaviors:
  1. Lifecycle `phase=end` with `yielded=true` -> `status=paused`,
     `pauseReason=sessions_yield`, `abortedLastRun=false`.
  2. Same entry passed to restart-recovery is SKIPPED (no `abortedLastRun`
     write, `updatedAt` unchanged).
  3. Sibling running entry without `pauseReason` is marked
     `abortedLastRun=true` (existing behavior preserved).
  Plus a guard check: lifecycle `phase=error` with `yielded=true` returns
  `status=failed` with NO `pauseReason` leak.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  === PR #75662 real-behavior proof ===
  host=rh-bot.lan pid=84884 node=v25.8.2
  dist=/Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist

  [1] lifecycle bundle: /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/server-chat-D9hpq-gf.js
  [1a] yielded terminal -> {"updatedAt":1780285520789,"status":"paused","startedAt":1780285519789,"endedAt":1780285520789,"runtimeMs":1000,"abortedLastRun":false,"pauseReason":"sessions_yield"}
  [1a] OK: paused status carries pauseReason=sessions_yield
  [1b] non-yielded terminal -> {"updatedAt":1780285520789,"status":"done","startedAt":1780285519789,"endedAt":1780285520789,"runtimeMs":1000,"abortedLastRun":false}
  [1b] OK: non-yielded run preserves status=done with no pauseReason
  [1c] error+yielded -> {"updatedAt":1780285520789,"status":"failed","startedAt":1780285519789,"endedAt":1780285520789,"runtimeMs":1000,"abortedLastRun":false}
  [1c] OK: error phase masks yielded marker, no pauseReason leak

  [2] seeded /var/folders/cn/.../state/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  [2] recovery bundle: /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/main-session-restart-recovery-CirHJY3p.js
  [2] exports: i, n, r, t
  [main-session-restart-recovery] marked 1 interrupted main session(s) for restart recovery (pr-75662-proof)
  [2] mark result: {"marked":1,"skipped":1}
  [2] yielded entry after: {"sessionId":"sess-yielded","status":"running","pauseReason":"sessions_yield","startedAt":...,"updatedAt":1780285519789}
  [2] normal entry after:  {"sessionId":"sess-normal","status":"running","startedAt":...,"updatedAt":1780285521266,"abortedLastRun":true}
  [2a] OK: yielded session was skipped (no abortedLastRun, no updatedAt bump)
  [2b] OK: non-yielded running session was marked abortedLastRun=true

  === ALL PROOFS PASSED ===
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: All three scenarios pass on the deployed bundle. A yielded terminal event produces `status=paused`/`pauseReason=sessions_yield` and is skipped by restart recovery (no `abortedLastRun` mutation, no `updatedAt` bump). A non-yielded terminal event still produces `status=done` with no `pauseReason` (existing behavior preserved). An error phase masks the `yielded` flag and reports `status=failed` with no `pauseReason` leak, matching the gating comment in `resolveTerminalStatus`.
- **Targeted tests**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts` -> 22 passed + 15 passed (37/37). Broader: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts` -> 172/172 passed. `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --only=core --threads=4` -> clean.
- **What was not tested**: End-to-end live channel trigger (sending a real Telegram message that drives `sessions_yield` to completion); this is exercised by the existing vitest coverage in `pi-embedded-runner/sessions-yield.orchestration.test.ts` rather than reproduced live on rh-bot.


## Live exact-head capture (2026-06-12): found and fixed an emit-side gap

Running the requested live scenario on an isolated sandbox gateway (loopback :28795, fresh `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, Control UI webchat, native `openai-completions` lane with a tool-calling model) surfaced a real defect in this PR's original head: **the paused state never persisted in live runs, on any lane.** A store probe polling every 0.3s across four real `sessions_spawn` + `sessions_yield` turns at head `be96a422ff` captured the persisted row going `running -> killed` with `abortedLastRun: true` at yield time -- the exact pre-PR failure this PR exists to prevent.

**Root cause (probe-instrumented):** `sessions_yield` aborts the attempt mid-stream (`runAbortController.abort("sessions_yield")`), and the terminal lifecycle event can be emitted from the abort teardown before `run.ts` post-processing writes the `yielded` meta. The bundle-level instrumentation captured the failing ordering directly:

```
emitLifecycleTerminal state.yielded = undefined  aborted = false   <- event emitted first
meta.yielded set -> false                                          <- run.ts wrote 551ms later
```

The event reaches `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` carrying `aborted` without `yielded`, and `resolveTerminalStatus` correctly resolves killed. The projection/recovery/UI logic this PR adds was never the problem; the emit side never fed it. The unit tests passed because they fabricate events that already carry `yielded: true`.

**Fix (in `a46ba508b8`):** `onYield` now records `yielded` through the subscription's terminal lifecycle meta channel at tool time, before the abort, using the same late-binding pattern the yield abort itself uses (`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`). A regression test pins the handler half: an end event with `state.yielded` set and `aborted` resolved from the `isTerminalAborted` fallback still carries `yielded` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.lifecycle.test.ts`).

**Post-fix live proof, end to end (same sandbox, instrumented bundle):**

```
10:27:40.065  [PROBE] meta.yielded set -> true              (onYield records at tool time)
10:27:40.423  [PROBE] emitLifecycleTerminal state.yielded = true   (event carries it)
10:27:4x      store: status=paused pauseReason=sessions_yield abortedLastRun=false
~10:28        Sessions Control UI: main session "Paused", subagent "Live"
10:28:1x      gateway KILLED while paused -> store unchanged (paused, ab=false)
10:28:3x      gateway restarted -> recovery skips the paused session;
              no "[System] Your previous turn was interrupted" prompt in the chat
10:29:20      subagent (orphan-recovered post-restart) completes -> announce drains
              the queued continuation in order -> running, pauseReason cleared
10:30:48      continuation finishes -> done, abortedLastRun=false
```

Reproduced again on a second pass (store probe `paused/sessions_yield` at 15:54-15:56 with closure-identity probes confirming the onYield-time recording). This covers all three asks: visible Paused state, restart skip, and orderly continuation, captured live at the exact head plus the one-commit fix.

**Codex lane now covered too (was a sibling-lane gap, now fixed in this PR).** The Codex app-server harness lane emits its own terminal lifecycle event (`extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` `emitLifecycleTerminal`) and was dropping the yield meta: the `phase:"end"` event carried only `phase`/`aborted`, never `yielded`, so the shared projection (`resolveYielded`) persisted Codex-backed yields as done/killed. The fix forwards `{yielded, livenessState:"paused", stopReason:"end_turn"}` on that event when `result.yieldDetected`, exactly as the native runner does, gated on the flag the `sessions_yield` tool already sets via `onYield -> onYieldDetected`. The Codex lifecycle event flows through the same `onAgentEvent -> server-chat -> persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEvent -> resolveYielded` path as the native lane.

Proven live on the Codex lane: a real `openai/gpt-5.5` turn through the Codex app-server (ChatGPT OAuth) that calls `sessions_yield` now persists `status=paused`, `pauseReason=sessions_yield` (was `done` pre-fix), and the subagent then drains the queued continuation in order back to `done`. An integration test (`extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.yield-lifecycle.test.ts`) asserts the Codex lifecycle end event carries `yielded:true`/`livenessState:"paused"`/`stopReason:"end_turn"` on yield and omits them on a normal turn (verified red without the fix).

Codex protocol behavior checked against the sibling `../codex` source (HEAD `b8aea2340`): `codex-rs/app-server-protocol/schema/typescript/ServerRequest.ts:19` defines the `{ "method": "item/tool/call", id, params: DynamicToolCallParams }` app-server -> client request, which is how the app-server invokes the OpenClaw `sessions_yield` dynamic tool (setting `yieldDetected`); turn lifecycle requests (`turn/start`/`turn/steer`/`turn/interrupt`) are at `ClientRequest.ts:90`. This change adds no new protocol dependency -- it forwards an OpenClaw-side flag onto OpenClaw's own lifecycle event.
